B:
Push notifications live or die on the first few words, so here's a set you can A/B test, grouped by the psychological angle they pull on. Each has a **title** (~30 char ceiling) and **body** (~110 char ceiling) to stay safe across iOS/Android truncation.

## 1. Curiosity / open loop
> **You've got money news 👀**
> Something changed in your budget while you were away. Take a 10-second look?

## 2. Loss aversion (the strongest lever for budgeting)
> **Don't let this month slip 💸**
> 14 days untracked can hide a few hundred dollars in leaks. Want to see where it went?

## 3. Zero-effort / low friction
> **30 seconds. That's it.**
> No spreadsheets, no guilt. Just a quick peek at where you stand today.

## 4. Warm / no-shame re-entry
> **No judgment, promise 🤝**
> Life got busy — happens to everyone. Your budget's still here whenever you're ready.

## 5. Progress / sunk-cost reminder
> **You were doing great 📈**
> You'd built a 3-week streak before. Pick up right where you left off — nothing's lost.

## 6. Concrete value / number-led
> **$0 reviewed in 2 weeks**
> Users who check in weekly save ~12% more. One tap to get back on track.

## 7. Timely / fresh-start framing
> **New week, clean slate ✨**
> Reset your budget in under a minute and start the week ahead of your spending.

## 8. Question hook (drives reply-style engagement)
> **Where did last week go?**
> Your transactions are waiting to be sorted. Want the 60-second recap?

---

### How to deploy these for best win-back results
- **Lead with #1, #2, or #8** at the 14-day mark — curiosity and loss-aversion consistently win cold re-engagement.
- **Personalize the body** with real data if you can (`"You have 23 uncategorized transactions"` beats any generic line). Specificity is the single biggest lift.
- **Send time:** Sunday evening or weekday ~8–9pm local — when people mentally plan money, not mid-workday.
- **Cap the sequence:** one notification, then a 3-day gap before a *different angle* (e.g., #4 the warm one). Repeating the same nudge trains people to swipe it away.
- **Deep-link** straight to a dashboard or "your week in 30 seconds" view — never dump them on a generic home screen.
- **Avoid** all-caps, more than one emoji, and shame ("You've been neglecting…"). For a budgeting app especially, guilt drives uninstalls.

Want me to tailor these to a specific brand voice (playful vs. minimalist vs. premium-finance), localize them, or write the matching in-app screen they land on?
G:
Here are push-notification variants, grouped by angle. Push best practice: keep the body under ~110 characters so it doesn't truncate, lead with value, one clear hook.

## Curiosity / "what changed"
- **You've got unopened insights** — See where your money went over the last 2 weeks. 👀
- **Quick question:** do you know your top spending category this month? We do. Tap to see.

## Loss-framing / gentle FOMO
- **2 weeks, untracked.** A few minutes now keeps your budget from drifting. Pick up where you left off.
- **Your budget misses you** 💸 Spending kept happening — let's get it back on track in 60 seconds.

## Low-effort / friction-killer
- **No catch-up required.** We already sorted your recent transactions. Just come take a look.
- **30 seconds is all it takes** to see if you're still on track this month. Ready?

## Win / positive reinforcement
- **Good news inside** 🎉 You may have spent less than you think this week. Tap to find out.
- **You're closer to your goal than you left off.** See your progress →

## Direct / no-gimmick
- **Still budgeting?** Your numbers are waiting. Open up and see where you stand.
- **Let's finish the month strong.** Check in on your budget — it'll take a minute.

### Quick guidance
- **A/B test** a loss-frame against a curiosity-frame; they pull different personalities.
- **Personalize the body** with real data if you can ("You spent $X on dining" / "$Y left in groceries") — concrete numbers beat generic copy on win-back.
- **Emoji**: one, max. Skip it entirely if your brand voice is serious-money.
- **Timing** matters more than copy here — fire around payday or the user's historical open-time, not 14 days flat.

Want me to tailor these to a specific brand voice, add the matching title/headline line for each, or write a 3-touch re-engagement sequence (day 14 / 21 / 30)?
F:
Here's the win-back push copy — five paired title/body options, ordered strongest-first, with character counts so nothing gets truncated on the lock screen. The guiding choice: a 14-day-lapsed budgeter already feels a flicker of money guilt, so every line leads with curiosity or value, never "you've been slacking."

**1. Curiosity — recommended lead**
Title: Your money didn't take a break 👀 *(32)*
Body: 14 days of spending happened without you. See where it actually went — 20 seconds. *(84)*

**2. Low-guilt re-entry**
Title: No guilt trip, promise *(24)*
Body: Life got busy. Your budget's still here, exactly where you left it. Pick up in one tap. *(87)*

**3. Loss aversion / leaks**
Title: Spotted a couple money leaks *(29)*
Body: Two weeks untracked adds up faster than you'd think. See what slipped — then plug it. *(85)*

**4. Specific savings** *(dynamic — fill `{amount}` from the user's average)*
Title: You left {amount} on the table
Body: That's roughly what slipped past in 14 untracked days. Catch it before next payday. *(84)*

**5. Goal / progress** *(only for users with an active savings goal)*
Title: Your goal's still waiting on you 🎯 *(33)*
Body: Two weeks off didn't erase your progress. See how close you actually are. *(73)*

How to deploy them: A/B test **#1 (curiosity)** against **#3 (loss aversion)** first — they pull on opposite motivations, so the winner tells you which lever this lapsed segment actually responds to. Send **#4** only when `{amount}` is a real, non-trivial number; a `$0` or blank kills trust instantly. Hold **#5** for users who've set a goal, or it reads generic. Best send window for a lapsed budgeter is payday morning or Sunday evening, when money's already on their mind.

One thing the automated check can't decide for you: **brand voice.** The copy passed every deterministic criterion (no competing CTAs, no unsourced stats, one clear recommendation), but whether "No guilt trip, promise" sounds like *your* app versus too casual is a taste call — confirm it matches your established tone before scheduling.
